summary: Back at the World Cup 40 years after their sole appearance, sealing it via the inter-continental play-off (2-1 over Bolivia). Graham Arnold — who took Australia to the 2022 Round of 16 — brought the same blueprint: stubborn defensively, dangerous at set pieces, direct with the ball. A genuine banana skin (1-1 friendly with Spain).
style: Variations of a 4-4-2 (sometimes proper wingers, sometimes a diamond) with two No 9s (Al-Hamadi, Hussein) doing lots of sideways running to block central passes in a compact mid-block. Limited possession and low-risk build-up concentrated to the wings; set pieces are the main route to goal, with a real aerial threat from the two strikers.

counterReliance.note: Compact block, direct for territory, set-piece-led

strengths: Stubborn, disciplined mid-block (10 clean sheets in qualifying)
Deadly from set pieces — most of their play-off goals came from dead balls
Two-striker aerial threat that troubles attack-minded opponents
Direct, low-risk approach that's hard to break down (took 75+ minutes to crack Thailand, Indonesia, Jordan)
weaknesses: Very limited in possession — reliant on set pieces and a slice of luck to score
Underdogs of the group; little margin if the block is breached

keyPlayers.name: Jalal Hassan
keyPlayers.note: 35, debuted in 2011 — 10 clean sheets in qualifying, joint-most in the Asian confederation

keyPlayers.name: Ali Al-Hamadi
keyPlayers.note: Half of the two-No-9 pairing — aerial threat and a blocker of central passes out of possession

tactics: Sit compact, go direct for territory, and play for set pieces behind a stubborn block — exactly the formula Arnold rode to the knockouts with Australia. Don't expect champagne football, but they could be a real banana skin in a strong group.
notes: Arnold took over after Jesús Casas was sacked in March 2025; this is only his ~13th match in charge.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group I (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
